perturbation.original_text: h) "Change of Control" means any transaction or series of transactions wherein (a) the voting securities of Supplier outstanding immediately prior thereto cease to represent at least fifty percent (50%) of the combined voting power of the surviving entity immediately after such transaction or transactions; (b) the stockholders or equity holders of Supplier approve a plan of complete liquidation of Supplier, or an agreement for the sale or disposition by Supplier of all or substantially all of Supplier's assets, other than to an Affiliate; (c) a Third Party becomes the beneficial owner of fifty percent (50%) or more of the combined voting power of the outstanding securities of Supplier; or (d) substantially all of Supplier's business or assets which relate to this Agreement are sold or otherwise transferred to a Third Party;
perturbation.changed_text: h) "Change of Control" means any transaction or series of transactions wherein (a) the voting securities of Supplier outstanding immediately prior thereto cease to represent at least fifty percent (50%) of the combined voting power of the surviving entity immediately after such transaction or transactions; (b) the stockholders or equity holders of Supplier approve a plan of complete liquidation of Supplier, or an agreement for the sale or disposition by Supplier of all or substantially all of Supplier's assets, other than to an Affiliate; (c) a Third Party becomes the beneficial owner of twenty-five percent (25%) or more of the combined voting power of the outstanding securities of Supplier; or (d) substantially all of Supplier's business or assets which relate to this Agreement are sold or otherwise transferred to a Third Party;
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Change of Control' is modified such that a Third Party becoming the beneficial owner of only 25% (instead of 50%) of the Supplier's securities constitutes a Change of Control. This is contradicting to 19.2 Change of Control, where during the Term, Supplier will promptly notify Customer in writing if at any time a Change of Control shall occur as to Supplier, such notification to be given no later than fifteen (15) days following such Change of Control. It is conflicting since depending on the case, the notification term may be activated with only 25% of benefical ownership, causing different interpretaions.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 19.2 Change of Control During the Term, Supplier will promptly notify Customer in writing if at any time a Change of Control shall occur as to Supplier, such notification to be given no later than fifteen (15) days following such Change of Control. [* * *]
perturbation.justification: The modified definition of 'Change of Control' lowers the threshold for a Third Party's beneficial ownership from 50% to 25%. This directly contradicts section 19.2, which requires the Supplier to notify the Customer of a Change of Control. The change creates a conflict because a 25% ownership now triggers the notification requirement, which was previously triggered at 50%. This meets the criteria for 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' due to inconsistent definitions.

perturbation.original_text: Customer shall pay any Taxes and reimburse Supplier for any Taxes for which Customer is responsible but which have been paid by Supplier. Subject to compliance with laws, the Parties shall reasonably cooperate to eliminate or minimize the amount of any such Taxes imposed on the transactions contemplated in this Agreement. For clarity, Customer shall not be liable for any taxes incurred by the Supplier including, without limitation, income taxes, employment taxes, use taxes, and the like incurred by Supplier, or for any penalties or interest related to the failure of Supplier to collect sales, use, VAT or similar taxes.
perturbation.changed_text: Customer shall pay any Taxes and reimburse Supplier for any Taxes for which Customer is responsible but which have been paid by Supplier. Subject to compliance with laws, the Parties shall reasonably cooperate to eliminate or minimize the amount of any such Taxes imposed on the transactions contemplated in this Agreement. For clarity, Customer shall also be liable for the income taxes, employment taxes, use taxes, and the like incurred by Supplier if directly related to the manufacturing of the product, or for any penalties or interest related to the failure of Supplier to collect sales, use, VAT or similar taxes.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text in Section 9.3 makes the Customer liable for the Supplier's income, employment, and use taxes related to manufacturing. This contradicts the prior definition where customer is not liable for those taxes. If a tax penalty occur, it is not clear who is responsible for the payment of such penalty, creating in-text contradiction. The previous section of the sentence has a contradiction with the new definition.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9.3 Taxes The Fees shall be exclusive of any taxes, customs duties, levies and other charges applicable to the supply of the Product under this Agreement ("Taxes"). Customer shall pay any Taxes and reimburse Supplier for any Taxes for which Customer is responsible but which have been paid by Supplier. Subject to compliance with laws, the Parties shall reasonably cooperate to eliminate or minimize the amount of any such Taxes imposed on the transactions contemplated in this Agreement. For clarity, Customer shall also be liable for the income taxes, employment taxes, use taxes, and the like incurred by Supplier if directly related to the manufacturing of the product, or for any penalties or interest related to the failure of Supplier to collect sales, use, VAT or similar taxes.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that the Customer is not liable for Supplier's income, employment, use taxes, and similar taxes. The changed text introduces an exception where the Customer is liable for these taxes if they are directly related to manufacturing of the product. This creates a direct contradiction within section 9.3, making it unclear whether the customer is liable for the Supplier's taxes in some circumstances, which is inconsistent. This fulfills the 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: On expiration or earlier termination of this Agreement, unless otherwise instructed by Customer, Supplier shall, within [* * *], return to Customer all samples or other supplies of the Product (for which Supplier has been paid) in its possession or control in any form, with the exception of any samples such as retention samples that Supplier may be required to keep according to Applicable Law. The cost of returning any such supplies shall: [* * *]
perturbation.changed_text: On expiration or earlier termination of this Agreement, unless otherwise instructed by Customer, Supplier shall, within [* * *], return to Customer all samples or other supplies of the Product (for which Supplier has been paid) in its possession or control in any form, with the exception of any samples such as retention samples that Supplier may be required to keep according to Applicable Law. The cost of returning any such supplies shall be borne by the Supplier, unless Customer is terminating the agreement due to Supplier's breach, in which case Customer will pay for the return of such supplies..
perturbation.explanation: The cost for returning the supplies of the product is now conditional based on the reason for termination, if a breach is the cause, then customer is now paying for returning those supplies. It creates ambiguity in who pays for the cost depending on the cause of the termination, contradicting the initial statement of the cost. The contradiction occurs within Section 18.3, making it an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 18.3 Return of Samples On expiration or earlier termination of this Agreement, unless otherwise instructed by Customer, Supplier shall, within [* * *], return to Customer all samples or other supplies of the Product (for which Supplier has been paid) in its possession or control in any form, with the exception of any samples such as retention samples that Supplier may be required to keep according to Applicable Law. The cost of returning any such supplies shall be borne by the Supplier, unless Customer is terminating the agreement due to Supplier's breach, in which case Customer will pay for the return of such supplies..
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a conditional payment responsibility for returning samples based on the termination cause, directly contradicting the original text, which initially assigns this cost solely to the supplier. The initial statement that 'The cost of returning any such supplies shall be borne by the Supplier' is now conditional based on who terminated the contract, making it an in-text contradiction as defined in the Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction definition.
